description: WHO IMCI canonical scenarios for PocketTriage eval. Ground truth derived from the WHO IMCI Chart Booklet 2014.

scenarios.input: 11-month-old boy. Cough for 3 days. Now breathing fast — I counted 58 breaths in one minute. Chest is sucking in when he breathes. He is restless and refusing to drink.

expected.tier_rationale: Chest indrawing + general danger sign (refusing to drink) = severe pneumonia or very severe disease per IMCI Section 2.

scenarios.input: 2-year-old girl. Watery diarrhoea for 2 days, around 6 stools per day, no blood. She is restless and irritable, drinks eagerly when offered ORS, eyes look slightly sunken, skin pinch goes back slowly.

expected.tier_rationale: Two signs of some dehydration (restless+irritable, eyes sunken, drinks eagerly, skin pinch slow). No general danger sign. IMCI Section 3, Some dehydration.

scenarios.input: 4-year-old boy in malaria-endemic region (Anambra, Nigeria). Fever 38.8C for 2 days, RDT positive for P. falciparum. No stiff neck, alert, no general danger signs, no other illness.

expected.tier_rationale: Fever + RDT positive, no danger signs, no stiff neck = uncomplicated malaria. IMCI Section 4, Malaria.

scenarios.input: 3-year-old girl. Runny nose and cough for 2 days. Breathing rate 32 per minute, no chest indrawing, drinking well, playful, no fever, no other danger signs.

expected.tier_rationale: No fast breathing (threshold 40/min for 12mo–5y), no chest indrawing, no danger signs = cough or cold. IMCI Section 2, No pneumonia.

scenarios.input: 2-year-old girl. Mother says child has been less active and not eating well for two weeks. Diarrhoea on and off. CHEW has just taken a MUAC reading — attached photo shows the tape.

expected.tier_rationale: MUAC photo shows reading 10.8 cm in the RED zone (< 11.5 cm). WHO IMCI: severe acute malnutrition requires referral. IMCI Section 6, Malnutrition + IMCI Section 1, General danger signs.
